content:
Consider a continuous-time, real-valued signal $f(t)$ whose Fourier transform $F(\omega)=$$\mathop f\limits_{ - \infty }^\infty $$ f(t) \exp (-j \omega t) d t$ exists.

Which one of the following statements is always TRUE?
explanation: $$ \begin{array}{rlrl} F(\omega) & =\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-j \omega t} d t \\ \Rightarrow |F(\omega)| & =\left|\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f(t) e^{-j \omega t} d t\right| \leq \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left|f(t) e^{-j \omega t}\right| d t \\ \Rightarrow |F(\omega)| & \leq \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|f(t)| d t \end{array} $$

content: Let $$m(t)$$ be a strictly band-limited signal with bandwidth B and energy E. Assuming $${\omega _0} = 10B$$, the energy in the signal $$m(t)\cos {\omega _0}t$$ is
explanation:
Given that $m(t)$ is a bandlimited signal with bandwidth $B$ and energy $E$.

Let $m(t)$ be represented in frequency domain as shown below,

Energy of $m(t)$ will be,

$$ E=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int\limits_{-B}^B 1^2 d \omega=\frac{2 B}{2 \pi}=\frac{B}{\pi} $$

Let $y(t)=m(t) \cos \omega_0 t$

Given : $\omega_0=10 B$

So, $y(t)=m(t) \cos 10 B t$

Taking Fourier transform on both sides, we get

$$ Y(\omega)=\frac{1}{2}[M(\omega-10 B)+M(\omega+10 B)] $$

$Y(\omega)$ can be plotted as shown below,

Now, energy of $y(t)$ will be,

$$ \begin{aligned} & E_y=2\left[\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int\limits_{9 B}^{11 B} 1^2 d \omega\right] \\ & E_y=\frac{1}{\pi}[11 B-9 B]=\frac{2 B}{\pi} \end{aligned} $$

So, from equation (i) and (ii),

$$ E_y=\frac{E}{2} $$

Hence, the correct option is (B).

content:
The Fourier transform $$x(\omega )$$ of $$x(t) = {e^{ - {t^2}}}$$ is

Note : $$\int\limits_{ - \infty }^\infty {{e^{ - {y^2}}}dy = \sqrt \pi } $$
explanation:
The Fourier transform of a function $$x(t)$$ is given by the integral:

$$X(\omega) = \int\limits_{- \infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

Given the function $$x(t) = e^{-t^2}$$, we need to find its Fourier transform. The integral thus becomes:

$$X(\omega) = \int\limits_{- \infty}^{\infty} e^{-t^2} e^{-j\omega t} dt$$

Notice that this integrand can be rewritten by combining the exponents:

$$X(\omega) = \int\limits_{- \infty}^{\infty} e^{-t^2 - j\omega t} dt$$

We can complete the square in the exponent to make it simpler. Start by rewriting the term inside the exponent:

$$-t^2 - j\omega t = -(t^2 + j\omega t) = -\left( t^2 + j\omega t + \frac{\omega^2}{4} - \frac{\omega^2}{4} \right) = -\left( (t + \frac{j\omega}{2})^2 - \frac{\omega^2}{4} \right) $$

Thus the integral becomes:

$$X(\omega) = \int\limits_{- \infty}^{\infty} e^{-\left( (t + \frac{j\omega}{2})^2 - \frac{\omega^2}{4} \right)} dt$$

Breaking it up, we can factor out the exponential term that does not depend on $$t$$:

$$X(\omega) = e^{\frac{-\omega^2}{4}} \int\limits_{- \infty}^{\infty} e^{-(t + \frac{j\omega}{2})^2} dt$$

Since the integral is in the form of a Gaussian integral and we know that shifting the integration variable does not affect the value of the integral:

$$\int\limits_{- \infty}^{\infty} e^{-(t + \frac{j\omega}{2})^2} dt = \int\limits_{- \infty}^{\infty} e^{-y^2} dy = \sqrt{\pi}$$

Therefore,

$$X(\omega) = e^{\frac{-\omega^2}{4}} \sqrt{\pi}$$

So the Fourier transform of $$x(t) = e^{-t^2}$$ is:

$$X(\omega) = \sqrt{\pi} e^{-\frac{\omega^2}{4}}$$

Hence, the correct answer is:

Option C: $$\sqrt \pi {e^{ - {{{\omega ^2}} \over 4}}}$$

The Fourier transform of a real valued time signal has

Consider two continuous time signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ as shown below

If $X(f)$ denotes the Fourier transform of $x(t)$, then the Fourier transform of $y(t)$ is ______.
explanation:
Given : The two continuous time signals $x(t)$ and $y(t)$ as shown below.

If $\quad x(t) \stackrel{\mathrm{FT}}{\longleftrightarrow} X(f)$

Step 1 : Length of $x(t)$ is 1 and length of $y(t)$ is 4.

Step 2 : In this step we will perform time shifting operation.

Step 3 : we will perform amplitude reversal operation.

$$ \text { If } \begin{aligned} & y(t)=-x\left(\frac{t-2}{4}\right)=-x\left(\frac{t}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & x(t) \stackrel{\mathrm{FT}}{\longleftrightarrow} X(f) \\ & x\left(t-\frac{1}{2}\right) \stackrel{\mathrm{FT}}{\longleftrightarrow} e^{-\frac{j \omega}{2}} X(\omega) \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{aligned} & x\left(t-\frac{1}{2}\right) \stackrel{\mathrm{FT}}{\longleftrightarrow} e^{-\frac{j 2 \pi f}{2}} X(f) \\ & x\left(\frac{t}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \stackrel{\mathrm{FT}}{\longleftrightarrow} \frac{1}{\left|\frac{1}{4}\right|} e^{-j \pi 4 f} X(4 f) \\ & x\left(\frac{t}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \stackrel{\mathrm{FT}}{\longleftrightarrow} 4 e^{-j 4 \pi f} X(4 f) \\ & -x\left(\frac{t}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \stackrel{\mathrm{FT}}{\longleftrightarrow}-4 e^{-j 4 \pi f} X(4 f) \end{aligned}\\ &\text { Hence the correct option is (B). } \end{aligned} $$

content: $X(\omega)$ is the Fourier transform of $x(t)$ shown below. The value of $\int\limits_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2 d \omega$ (rounded off to two decimal places) is $\_\_\_\_$ .
explanation:
$$ \text { Given : Signal } x(t) \text { is shown in figure. } $$

Method 1

Using Rayleigh energy theorem,

Energy of $x(t)$ is given by,

$$ \begin{array}{ll} & E_x=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|x(t)|^2 d t=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2 d \omega \\ \therefore & I=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2 d \omega=2 \pi E_x \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{array} $$

To find energy of $x(t)$, we can consider $x(t)$ to be combination of signals $x_1(t), x_2(t), x_3(t)$ and $x_4(t)$ as shown in figure. $x_1$ and $x_2$ are non overlapping with $x_3$ and $x_4$ but $x_3$ and $x_4$ are overlapping with each other.

$$ E_x=E\left[x_1(t)\right]+E\left[x_2(t)\right]+E\left[\text { Combination of } x_3(t) \text { and } x_4(t)\right]\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii) $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\begin{array}{ll} E_{x_3}=A^2 \tau & E_{x_4}=\frac{2 A^2 \tau}{3} \\ E_{x_3}=1 \times 2=2 & E_{x_4}=\frac{2 \times 4 \times 1}{3}=\frac{8}{3} \end{array}\\ &\text { Energy in combination of } x_3(t) \text { and } x_4(t)=E_{x_3}+E_{x_4}+2 \text { [Area under function } x_3(t) \cdot x_4(t) \text { ] } \end{aligned} $$

From figure, $\quad x_3(t) \cdot x_4(t)=x_4(t)$

Area

$$ \left[x_3(t) \cdot x_4(t)\right]=\text { Area }\left[x_4(t)\right]=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times 2=2 $$

Energy in combination of $x_3(t)$ and $x_4(t)=2+\frac{8}{3}+(2 \times 2)=6+\frac{8}{3}$

From equation (ii), energy of $x(t)$ is

$$ E_x=\frac{1}{3}+\frac{1}{3}+6+\frac{8}{3}=\frac{1+1+18+8}{3}=\frac{28}{3} $$

From equation (i), value of given integral

$$ I=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2 d \omega=2 \pi \times \frac{28}{3}=58.64 $$

Hence, the value of integral is 58.64 .

Method 2

Given : Signal $x(t)$ is shown in figure,

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Using Raleigh energy theorem, energy of } x(t)\\ &\begin{aligned} & E_x=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|x(t)|^2 d t=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2 d \omega \\ & I=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2 d \omega=2 \pi E_x=2 \pi \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|x(t)|^2 d t \\ & \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}|X(\omega)|^2 d \omega=2 \pi \times \int_{-1}^3|x(t)|^2 d t=2 \pi \times 2 \int_{-1}^1|x(t)|^2 d t \\ & E_x=4 \pi\left[\int_{-1}^0|x(t)|^2 d t+\int_0^1|x(t)|^2 d t\right] \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(i)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Equation of line in region }(-1,0) \text { is given by, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y-y_1=\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\left(x-x_1\right) \\ & y-0=\frac{1-0}{0-(-1)}(x-(-1)) \\ & y=x+1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(ii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

$$ \begin{aligned} &\text { Equation of line in region }(0,1) \text { is given by, }\\ &\begin{aligned} & y-y_1=\frac{y_2-y_1}{x_2-x_1}\left(x-x_1\right) \\ & y-1=\frac{3-1}{1-0}(x-0) \\ & y-1=2 x \\ & y=2 x+1 \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,...(iii)\end{aligned} \end{aligned} $$

Put the value from equation (ii) and (iii) in equation (i),

$$ \begin{aligned} & E_x=4 \pi\left[\int_{-1}^0(x+1)^2 d x+\int_0^1(2 x+1)^2 d x\right] \\ & E_x=4 \pi\left[\int_{-1}^0\left(x^2+2 x+1\right) d x+\int_{-1}^0\left(4 x^2+4 x+1\right) d x\right] \\ & E_x=4 \pi\left[\left(\frac{x^3}{3}+\frac{2 x^2}{2}+x\right)_{-1}^0+\left(\frac{4 x^3}{3}+\frac{4 x^2}{2}+x\right)_0^1\right] \\ & E_x=4 \pi\left[\left(\frac{1}{3}-1+1\right)+\left(\frac{4}{3}+2+1\right)\right] \\ & E_x=4 \pi\left[\frac{1}{3}+\frac{13}{3}\right] \\ & E_x=4 \pi \times \frac{14}{3} \\ & E_x=58.64 \\ & E_x=\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\left|x(\omega)^2\right| d \omega=58.64 \end{aligned} $$

Hence, the value of integral is 58.64 .
